does: This paper introduces Contrastive Distribution Matching (CDM), a method that learns amortized twist functions for Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) sampling in discrete diffusion models. The core contribution is reformulating the gradient estimator to exploit closed-form forward kernels in discrete diffusion, enabling efficient training of a parameterized twist function using positive and negative contrastive samples. The method addresses the computational bottleneck of inference-time twist estimation by precomputing and amortizing this cost during training. Empirical results demonstrate that CDM achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines while adding less than 5% computational overhead relative to a single forward pass through the base model.
connects: This work builds on Twisted Sequential Monte Carlo, which provides asymptotically exact sampling from reward-tilted distributions but suffers from expensive online twist function estimation in high-dimensional discrete spaces. The paper extends prior research on amortized inference and neural proposal functions for Monte Carlo methods by combining contrastive learning with the specific structure of discrete diffusion models. It responds to the practical limitations of existing exact SMC approaches by leveraging the mathematical properties of discrete diffusion forward processes, connecting to broader efforts in structured sampling, importance weighting, and learned proposal optimization that have emerged from variational inference and reinforcement learning communities.
enables: This framework opens pathways for efficient sampling from complex reward-tilted distributions in downstream applications like structured generation with reward constraints, controlled text or molecule generation, and sample-efficient reinforcement learning with discrete action spaces. The amortized approach suggests future work on scaling SMC methods to higher-dimensional discrete spaces and more complex diffusion architectures. The contrastive learning formulation could inspire hybrid methods combining neural proposal learning with other exact inference techniques, and the closed-form kernel exploitation may generalize to other structured generative models beyond discrete diffusion.
